title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
Nextflow is a workflow DSL that makes it easy to write scalable and reproducible pipelines that run locally or on cloud providers (GCP, AWS). It integrates well with containers, Conda, R and Python tools, and supports parallel execution out of the box.






Important



This guide focuses on practical, runnable examples: local testing, GCP and AWS execution, and integrating R/Python code. Replace example placeholders (project IDs, bucket names, IAM ARNs) with your values.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
Nextflow is a workflow DSL that makes it easy to write scalable and reproducible pipelines that run locally or on cloud providers (GCP, AWS). It integrates well with containers, Conda, R and Python tools, and supports parallel execution out of the box.






Important



This guide focuses on practical, runnable examples: local testing, GCP and AWS execution, and integrating R/Python code. Replace example placeholders (project IDs, bucket names, IAM ARNs) with your values.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
section: 2. Quick Start — install and run locally
text:
2. Quick Start — install and run locally

Requirements

Java 11+ (Nextflow runs on the JVM)
nextflow CLI (install via curl)
Optional: Docker or Conda for reproducible software environments

# Install Nextflow (Linux/macOS)
curl -s https://get.nextflow.io | bash
# Move to PATH (example)
sudo mv nextflow /usr/local/bin/

# Verify
nextflow -v


Hello-world workflow (main.nf)
#!/usr/bin/env nextflow

process sayHello {
  output:
    file 'hello.txt'

  script:
  """
  echo "Hello from Nextflow" > hello.txt
  """
}

workflow {
  sayHello()
}
Run locally:
nextflow run main.nf -profile standard
ls -l work/
cat work/*/hello.txt

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
3. Core Concepts (brief)

process: computational step (command/script) with input/output.
channel: stream of data connecting processes.
workflow: organizes processes and channels.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
section: 4. Running Nextflow on Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
text:
4. Running Nextflow on Google Cloud Platform (GCP)

Overview
We demonstrate using GCP Compute + Cloud Storage. For larger work, you can use Nextflow Tower, Google Genomics Pipelines, or compute clusters (GKE).


Prerequisites

gcloud installed and authenticated
A GCP project and billing enabled
A GCS bucket for workspace/storage

# Set project and region
gcloud config set project YOUR_PROJECT_ID
gcloud config set compute/region us-central1
# Create bucket (example)
gsutil mb -l us-central1 gs://your-nextflow-bucket


Nextflow config for GCP (example nextflow.config)
profiles {
  gcp {
    process.executor = 'google-lifesciences'  // uses Google Life Sciences API
    google {
      project = 'YOUR_PROJECT_ID'
      region  = 'us-central1'
      auth = '/path/to/google-credentials.json'   // or use ADC
      bucket = 'gs://your-nextflow-bucket'
    }
    workDir = 'gs://your-nextflow-bucket/work'
  }
}

Authentication: prefer Application Default Credentials (ADC). To create a service account and key:

gcloud iam service-accounts create nf-runner --display-name "Nextflow runner"
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding YOUR_PROJECT_ID \
  --member="serviceAccount:nf-runner@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
  --role="roles/storage.admin"
gcloud iam service-accounts keys create key.json \
  --iam-account nf-runner@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com
export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="$(pwd)/key.json"


Run on GCP
nextflow run main.nf -profile gcp -with-report report.html -with-trace trace.txt


Cost & monitoring

Store intermediate files in GCS to avoid expensive disk.
Use small VM images for lightweight tasks.
Monitor via Cloud Console and check gcloud billing reports.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
6. Integrating R and Python

Containerization (recommended)

Use Docker images with R and Python environments, or use Conda with Nextflow’s conda support.

Example process using Python:
process runPython {
  container 'python:3.11-slim'
  input:
    path x
  output:
    path 'out.txt'

  script:
  """
  python -c 'print("Processing", "$x")' > out.txt
  """
}
Example conda usage:
process analysis {
  conda 'envs/env.yml'
  script:
  """
  Rscript analysis.R input.csv output.csv
  """
}
envs/env.yml (example)
name: nf-env
channels:
  - conda-forge
dependencies:
  - r-base=4.2
  - r-essentials
  - python=3.11
  - pandas


R script example (analysis.R)
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)
dat <- read.csv(args[1])
summary <- summary(dat)
write.csv(as.data.frame(summary), args[2], row.names=FALSE)


Passing data between R and Python

Use files in workDir and channels to pass file paths.
Keep intermediate files in object storage (S3/GCS) when running in cloud.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
7. Practical Bioinformatics Example (FASTQ QC & alignment)

Process 1: split/validate FASTQ
Process 2: run fastp or fastqc
Process 3: align with bwa or minimap2
Use containers for exact tool versions and pin references.

Small example process:
process fastqc {
  container 'biocontainers/fastqc:v0.11.9_cv8'
  input:
    path fastq
  output:
    path "*.html"
  """
  fastqc $fastq -o .
  """
}

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
8. Clinical Trials / Regulatory Considerations

Enforce data access controls: use private buckets and VPCs.
Log and version workflows: enable reports and execution metadata.
Separate PHI/PII and use encryption at rest/in transit.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
9. Best Practices

Containerize reproducible environments (Docker/Conda).
Test workflows locally before the cloud.
Use small sample datasets for CI tests.
Keep credentials out of repo; use secret managers or ADC.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
10. References & Next Steps

Nextflow docs: https://www.nextflow.io/docs/
Nextflow + Google: https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/GoogleCloud.html
Nextflow + AWS: https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/aws/index.html

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:
This page teaches you Quarto step-by-step. It is built for people who are new to Quarto and may not have any background in R.






Important



You do not need R to use Quarto. Start with plain Markdown and HTML output, then add code later if you want.




Quarto is an open-source publishing system that turns plain text into polished documents and websites.

Write content in Markdown
Add headings, lists, links, images, and tables
Render to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more




Quarto helps you:

create one document and output it in multiple formats
keep text and code together in one file
build websites, tutorials, and reports
publish clean content with version control




No.
Quarto works with:

plain Markdown only
Python
R
Julia
Observable JavaScript

For beginners, start with text-only documents.






Quarto
A text editor such as VS Code





Python or R if you want executable code blocks
A TeX engine like TinyTeX or TeX Live for PDF output




quarto --version
If Quarto is installed, this prints a version number.




Markdown is a simple text format that makes writing easy.


# Main title
## Section title
### Smaller section



**bold**
*italic*



- item one
- item two



[Quarto website](https://quarto.org)




Create a file named hello.qmd and add:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start



The section between --- lines is YAML. It sets the document title, format, and other options.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
format: html
---



Run this command:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto generates hello.html.



Quarto websites use multiple .qmd files and a _quarto.yml project file.
Example _quarto.yml:
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Quarto Website"
  navbar:
    right:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: tutorial.qmd
        text: Tutorial

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
Example index.qmd:
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.





![A sample image](images/example.png)



| Tool   | Purpose          |
|--------|------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents |
| VS Code| Edit files       |




You can add code blocks that only show code:
quarto render hello.qmd
If you install Python or R, Quarto can also run code and show results.




Code
print("Hello from Python")


Hello from Python






You can render HTML, PDF, and Word from one file.
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default




Forgetting the --- lines in YAML
Using the wrong file extension (.qmd instead of .txt)
Broken image paths
Trying to run code without installing Python or R





Install Quarto
Create a .qmd file
Add YAML and Markdown
Render to HTML
View the output
Edit and render again




Try these next steps:

Add another page like about.qmd
Use a custom theme or CSS
Add images, tables, and links
Experiment with format: docx or format: pdf


Great! Your Quarto website now contains a full beginner tutorial with examples and step-by-step guidance.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Welcome! This tutorial is written for people who are completely new to Quarto and may also have no background in R.
Quarto is a tool for creating: - reports - blogs - websites - books - presentations - PDFs and Word documents
You write content in simple plain text using Markdown, and Quarto turns it into polished output.



Quarto is an open-source publishing system.
In simple words: - You write text in a file - You can add headings, lists, images, tables, and code - Quarto converts that file into HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more
You do not need to know R to start using Quarto.
Quarto can work with: - plain Markdown only - Python - R - Julia - Observable JavaScript
If you are a beginner, you can first learn Quarto as a document writing tool without writing any code.




Quarto is useful because it helps you: - write once and create multiple output formats - keep text, code, and results in one place - create professional-looking documents - make reproducible reports - build personal websites, course notes, and books


You can use Quarto to create: - class notes - project reports - resumes - research papers - tutorials - data analysis reports - technical blogs - slide presentations





No.
This is very important.
You can use Quarto without knowing R.
There are two common ways to use Quarto:


You write Markdown and create a document. No programming needed.



Later, if you want, you can add Python or R code blocks.
So if you are a complete beginner, start with text-only documents.





To use Quarto comfortably, install the following:



Quarto
A text editor or IDE





VS Code (great for beginners)
RStudio / Positron (optional, even if you do not use R)





Python if you want to run Python code later
R if you want to run R code later
TinyTeX or TeX Live if you want to create PDFs




Go to the official site: - https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Download and install Quarto for your operating system.



Open your terminal and run:
quarto --version
If Quarto is installed correctly, you will see a version number.





Markdown is a very simple way to format text.
Instead of clicking buttons for bold headings or lists, you type small symbols.




# Main Title
## Section Title
### Smaller Section



**bold text**
*italic text*



- item one
- item two
- item three



1. First step
2. Second step
3. Third step



[Quarto Website](https://quarto.org)
This is one reason Quarto is beginner-friendly.






Quarto documents usually use the .qmd extension.
Create a file called:
hello.qmd
Add this content:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

## About Me

I am learning Quarto.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start


That section is called YAML.
It stores document settings like: - title - author - date - output format
In this example: - title sets the title of the document - format: html tells Quarto to create an HTML page





To convert a .qmd file into output, Quarto uses the word render.


Run:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto will create an HTML file, usually:
hello.html
Open that file in your browser.



If you use VS Code with Quarto installed, you can often preview or render directly from the editor.





A simple Quarto document has two main parts:


This controls document settings.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-06-12"
format: html
---



This is where you write: - headings - paragraphs - lists - images - tables - code blocks







# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3



Just write normal text.



Leave a blank line between paragraphs.



**bold**
*italic*
`code`



> This is an important note.



---



- apple
- banana
- orange



1. Install Quarto
2. Create a file
3. Render the file





You can add an image like this:
![A sample image](images/example.png)
Explanation: - text inside [] is alternate text - text inside () is the image path
If your image is in a folder called images, the file path might be: - images/photo.jpg - images/chart.png




[Visit Quarto](https://quarto.org)
This creates a clickable link.




Simple Markdown table:
| Tool   | Purpose            |
|--------|--------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents   |
| VS Code| Edit files         |
| Python | Run code optional  |
This becomes a formatted table in the output.




Even if you do not know programming yet, it is helpful to understand the idea.
A code block is a section where you write code.


```bash
quarto render hello.qmd

This only displays code.

### Executable code block
If you have Python or R installed, Quarto can run code blocks and show results.

Example with Python:

````markdown
```python
print("Hello from Python")

If Python is available, Quarto can run it and include the output.

Again: **this is optional** for beginners.

---

## 14. A text-only beginner example

Create a file named `intro.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Introduction to Quarto"
format: html
---

# Welcome

Quarto helps you create beautiful documents.

## What I learned

- Quarto uses `.qmd` files
- Markdown is easy to write
- I can create HTML pages
- I do not need R to begin

## My Next Step

I will learn how to add images and tables.
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render intro.qmd
```

---

## 15. Creating different output formats

One powerful feature of Quarto is that you can generate different types of output.

### HTML
```yaml
format: html
```

### PDF
```yaml
format: pdf
```

### Word
```yaml
format: docx
```

### Multiple formats
```yaml
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default
```

Then run:

```bash
quarto render yourfile.qmd
```

### Important note about PDF
Creating PDFs usually requires a LaTeX installation such as TinyTeX.

If PDF rendering fails, start with HTML first.

---

## 16. Working with sections

A bigger document can be organized into sections.

Example:

```markdown
# Chapter 1
## Topic 1.1
## Topic 1.2

# Chapter 2
## Topic 2.1
```

This structure helps readers navigate your content.

In HTML output, Quarto may also generate a table of contents.

---

## 17. Adding a table of contents

You can enable it in YAML:

```yaml
---
title: "My Tutorial"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---
```

This adds a table of contents automatically.

---

## 18. Changing theme and appearance

For HTML documents, you can choose themes.

Example:

```yaml
---
title: "Styled Document"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
---
```

Some common themes:
- cosmo
- litera
- flatly
- darkly
- minty

You can experiment and see which one you like.

---

## 19. Creating a presentation

Quarto can create slides too.

Example:

```markdown
---
title: "My First Presentation"
format: revealjs
---

# Slide 1
Welcome to my presentation.

# Slide 2
- Point one
- Point two
```

Render it and Quarto will create a slide deck.

---

## 20. Creating a website

Quarto can also build websites.

A basic website project often includes:
- `_quarto.yml`
- `index.qmd`
- other `.qmd` pages

Example `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Website"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
```

Example `index.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.
```

This is more advanced, but it shows how powerful Quarto is.

---

## 21. What is `_quarto.yml`?

When you work on a Quarto project with multiple files, `_quarto.yml` is the main configuration file.

It can define:
- project type
- website settings
- book settings
- output format defaults
- themes
- navigation

Think of it as the project control file.

---

## 22. How Quarto is different from Word

In Word, you usually format things visually.

In Quarto, you write simple plain text with lightweight formatting.

### Benefits of Quarto
- easier version control with Git and GitHub
- reusable documents
- cleaner structure
- can combine writing and code
- easier to publish online

At first it may feel unfamiliar, but many people find it easier over time.

---

## 23. Beginner workflow

Here is a simple beginner workflow:

1. Install Quarto
2. Create a `.qmd` file
3. Write Markdown content
4. Add YAML at the top
5. Render the file to HTML
6. Open the output in a browser
7. Edit and render again

This is enough to get started.

---

## 24. Common beginner mistakes

### Mistake 1: Forgetting the YAML lines
YAML must start and end with `---`.

### Mistake 2: Wrong file extension
Use `.qmd`, not `.txt`.

### Mistake 3: PDF not rendering
This often happens because LaTeX is not installed.

### Mistake 4: Broken image path
Make sure the image file path is correct.

### Mistake 5: Expecting code to run without installing Python or R
If you want executable code blocks, you need the related language installed.

---

## 25. Example: A small personal profile page

Create `profile.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "My Profile"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

# About Me

My name is Praveen.

I am learning Quarto to create documents and websites.

## Skills I Want to Learn

- Quarto
- Markdown
- GitHub
- Python

## Favorite Resources

- [Quarto](https://quarto.org)
- [Markdown Guide](https://www.markdownguide.org)
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render profile.qmd
```

---

## 26. Example: A study notes document

```markdown
---
title: "Study Notes"
format: html
---

# Chapter 1

This chapter explains the basics.

## Key Points

- Point A
- Point B
- Point C

## Summary

This chapter introduced the main ideas.
```

This is useful for students and self-learners.

---

## 27. If you later want to learn code in Quarto

Once you are comfortable with text and Markdown, you can gradually add code.

### Python example

````markdown
```python
name = "Praveen"
print("Hello", name)
```



```r
x <- 10
x + 5
```
But remember: learning Quarto does not require learning R first.






Start with HTML output
Write short documents first
Focus on Markdown before advanced features
Keep files organized in folders
Use meaningful file names
Render often to check your output
Learn one feature at a time





Here is a beginner-friendly order:


Learn: - headings - paragraphs - bold and italic - lists - links



Learn: - YAML title and format - rendering to HTML - adding images - adding tables



Learn: - themes - table of contents - multiple pages - websites



Optional: - Python code blocks - R code blocks - PDFs - presentations - books







No. Anyone can use it for writing and publishing.



No. You can start with plain text and Markdown.



Yes, Quarto is open source and free to use.



Yes.



Yes.



No. VS Code works very well too.





Before starting, make sure you can do these:

install Quarto
create a .qmd file
write a title in YAML
add headings and lists
render to HTML
open the output file

If you can do these, you are already using Quarto successfully.




Quarto is a beginner-friendly publishing tool.
Even if you: - do not know R - do not know programming - have never written Markdown before
You can still start learning Quarto today.
The best way to learn is: - create a small .qmd file - write simple content - render it - improve it step by step
Start simple, and build confidence gradually.




Try this today:

Install Quarto
Create hello.qmd
Paste this content:

---
title: "Hello Quarto"
format: html
---

# My First Page

I am learning Quarto.

- I can write text
- I can create headings
- I can render HTML

Run:

quarto render hello.qmd
That is your first Quarto success.

If you want, the next tutorial can be about: - Quarto for complete beginners using VS Code - How to create a Quarto website step by step - Markdown for absolute beginners - Quarto with Python for beginners

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Quarto is an open-source publishing system.
In simple words: - You write text in a file - You can add headings, lists, images, tables, and code - Quarto converts that file into HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more
You do not need to know R to start using Quarto.
Quarto can work with: - plain Markdown only - Python - R - Julia - Observable JavaScript
If you are a beginner, you can first learn Quarto as a document writing tool without writing any code.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Quarto is useful because it helps you: - write once and create multiple output formats - keep text, code, and results in one place - create professional-looking documents - make reproducible reports - build personal websites, course notes, and books


You can use Quarto to create: - class notes - project reports - resumes - research papers - tutorials - data analysis reports - technical blogs - slide presentations

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
No.
This is very important.
You can use Quarto without knowing R.
There are two common ways to use Quarto:


You write Markdown and create a document. No programming needed.



Later, if you want, you can add Python or R code blocks.
So if you are a complete beginner, start with text-only documents.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
To use Quarto comfortably, install the following:



Quarto
A text editor or IDE





VS Code (great for beginners)
RStudio / Positron (optional, even if you do not use R)





Python if you want to run Python code later
R if you want to run R code later
TinyTeX or TeX Live if you want to create PDFs




Go to the official site: - https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Download and install Quarto for your operating system.



Open your terminal and run:
quarto --version
If Quarto is installed correctly, you will see a version number.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Markdown is a very simple way to format text.
Instead of clicking buttons for bold headings or lists, you type small symbols.




# Main Title
## Section Title
### Smaller Section



**bold text**
*italic text*



- item one
- item two
- item three



1. First step
2. Second step
3. Third step



[Quarto Website](https://quarto.org)
This is one reason Quarto is beginner-friendly.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Quarto documents usually use the .qmd extension.
Create a file called:
hello.qmd
Add this content:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

## About Me

I am learning Quarto.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start


That section is called YAML.
It stores document settings like: - title - author - date - output format
In this example: - title sets the title of the document - format: html tells Quarto to create an HTML page

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
To convert a .qmd file into output, Quarto uses the word render.


Run:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto will create an HTML file, usually:
hello.html
Open that file in your browser.



If you use VS Code with Quarto installed, you can often preview or render directly from the editor.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
A simple Quarto document has two main parts:


This controls document settings.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-06-12"
format: html
---



This is where you write: - headings - paragraphs - lists - images - tables - code blocks

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3



Just write normal text.



Leave a blank line between paragraphs.



**bold**
*italic*
`code`



> This is an important note.



---



- apple
- banana
- orange



1. Install Quarto
2. Create a file
3. Render the file

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
You can add an image like this:
![A sample image](images/example.png)
Explanation: - text inside [] is alternate text - text inside () is the image path
If your image is in a folder called images, the file path might be: - images/photo.jpg - images/chart.png

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
[Visit Quarto](https://quarto.org)
This creates a clickable link.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Simple Markdown table:
| Tool   | Purpose            |
|--------|--------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents   |
| VS Code| Edit files         |
| Python | Run code optional  |
This becomes a formatted table in the output.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Even if you do not know programming yet, it is helpful to understand the idea.
A code block is a section where you write code.


```bash
quarto render hello.qmd

This only displays code.

### Executable code block
If you have Python or R installed, Quarto can run code blocks and show results.

Example with Python:

````markdown
```python
print("Hello from Python")

If Python is available, Quarto can run it and include the output.

Again: **this is optional** for beginners.

---

## 14. A text-only beginner example

Create a file named `intro.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Introduction to Quarto"
format: html
---

# Welcome

Quarto helps you create beautiful documents.

## What I learned

- Quarto uses `.qmd` files
- Markdown is easy to write
- I can create HTML pages
- I do not need R to begin

## My Next Step

I will learn how to add images and tables.
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render intro.qmd
```

---

## 15. Creating different output formats

One powerful feature of Quarto is that you can generate different types of output.

### HTML
```yaml
format: html
```

### PDF
```yaml
format: pdf
```

### Word
```yaml
format: docx
```

### Multiple formats
```yaml
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default
```

Then run:

```bash
quarto render yourfile.qmd
```

### Important note about PDF
Creating PDFs usually requires a LaTeX installation such as TinyTeX.

If PDF rendering fails, start with HTML first.

---

## 16. Working with sections

A bigger document can be organized into sections.

Example:

```markdown
# Chapter 1
## Topic 1.1
## Topic 1.2

# Chapter 2
## Topic 2.1
```

This structure helps readers navigate your content.

In HTML output, Quarto may also generate a table of contents.

---

## 17. Adding a table of contents

You can enable it in YAML:

```yaml
---
title: "My Tutorial"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---
```

This adds a table of contents automatically.

---

## 18. Changing theme and appearance

For HTML documents, you can choose themes.

Example:

```yaml
---
title: "Styled Document"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
---
```

Some common themes:
- cosmo
- litera
- flatly
- darkly
- minty

You can experiment and see which one you like.

---

## 19. Creating a presentation

Quarto can create slides too.

Example:

```markdown
---
title: "My First Presentation"
format: revealjs
---

# Slide 1
Welcome to my presentation.

# Slide 2
- Point one
- Point two
```

Render it and Quarto will create a slide deck.

---

## 20. Creating a website

Quarto can also build websites.

A basic website project often includes:
- `_quarto.yml`
- `index.qmd`
- other `.qmd` pages

Example `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Website"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
```

Example `index.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.
```

This is more advanced, but it shows how powerful Quarto is.

---

## 21. What is `_quarto.yml`?

When you work on a Quarto project with multiple files, `_quarto.yml` is the main configuration file.

It can define:
- project type
- website settings
- book settings
- output format defaults
- themes
- navigation

Think of it as the project control file.

---

## 22. How Quarto is different from Word

In Word, you usually format things visually.

In Quarto, you write simple plain text with lightweight formatting.

### Benefits of Quarto
- easier version control with Git and GitHub
- reusable documents
- cleaner structure
- can combine writing and code
- easier to publish online

At first it may feel unfamiliar, but many people find it easier over time.

---

## 23. Beginner workflow

Here is a simple beginner workflow:

1. Install Quarto
2. Create a `.qmd` file
3. Write Markdown content
4. Add YAML at the top
5. Render the file to HTML
6. Open the output in a browser
7. Edit and render again

This is enough to get started.

---

## 24. Common beginner mistakes

### Mistake 1: Forgetting the YAML lines
YAML must start and end with `---`.

### Mistake 2: Wrong file extension
Use `.qmd`, not `.txt`.

### Mistake 3: PDF not rendering
This often happens because LaTeX is not installed.

### Mistake 4: Broken image path
Make sure the image file path is correct.

### Mistake 5: Expecting code to run without installing Python or R
If you want executable code blocks, you need the related language installed.

---

## 25. Example: A small personal profile page

Create `profile.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "My Profile"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

# About Me

My name is Praveen.

I am learning Quarto to create documents and websites.

## Skills I Want to Learn

- Quarto
- Markdown
- GitHub
- Python

## Favorite Resources

- [Quarto](https://quarto.org)
- [Markdown Guide](https://www.markdownguide.org)
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render profile.qmd
```

---

## 26. Example: A study notes document

```markdown
---
title: "Study Notes"
format: html
---

# Chapter 1

This chapter explains the basics.

## Key Points

- Point A
- Point B
- Point C

## Summary

This chapter introduced the main ideas.
```

This is useful for students and self-learners.

---

## 27. If you later want to learn code in Quarto

Once you are comfortable with text and Markdown, you can gradually add code.

### Python example

````markdown
```python
name = "Praveen"
print("Hello", name)
```



```r
x <- 10
x + 5
```
But remember: learning Quarto does not require learning R first.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Start with HTML output
Write short documents first
Focus on Markdown before advanced features
Keep files organized in folders
Use meaningful file names
Render often to check your output
Learn one feature at a time

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Here is a beginner-friendly order:


Learn: - headings - paragraphs - bold and italic - lists - links



Learn: - YAML title and format - rendering to HTML - adding images - adding tables



Learn: - themes - table of contents - multiple pages - websites



Optional: - Python code blocks - R code blocks - PDFs - presentations - books

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
No. Anyone can use it for writing and publishing.



No. You can start with plain text and Markdown.



Yes, Quarto is open source and free to use.



Yes.



Yes.



No. VS Code works very well too.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Before starting, make sure you can do these:

install Quarto
create a .qmd file
write a title in YAML
add headings and lists
render to HTML
open the output file

If you can do these, you are already using Quarto successfully.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Quarto is a beginner-friendly publishing tool.
Even if you: - do not know R - do not know programming - have never written Markdown before
You can still start learning Quarto today.
The best way to learn is: - create a small .qmd file - write simple content - render it - improve it step by step
Start simple, and build confidence gradually.

Install Quarto

---

format: html
---

# My First Page

- I can write text
- I can create headings

Run:

quarto render hello.qmd
That is your first Quarto success.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
text:
Imagine you bake a cake using a recipe. You write down every ingredient, every step, and every oven temperature. If someone else follows the exact same recipe, they should get the exact same cake.
In science and medicine, we face a similar problem with software:

One person runs an analysis on a laptop and gets one result.
Another person runs the “same” analysis on a different computer and gets a different result — because their software versions, settings, or operating system are not identical.

Docker solves this by packaging software — and everything it needs to run — into a standard box that works the same way on almost any computer.






Tip



Simple definition: Docker lets you put an application and all its dependencies into a portable box called a container, so it runs the same way everywhere.




Before modern shipping, loading a ship was messy — every crate was a different size and shape.
Then standard shipping containers arrived. Any crane, truck, or ship built for containers could move any container, anywhere in the world.
Docker does the same for software:







Real world
Docker world




Shipping container
Container (running app)


Blueprint for the container
Image (frozen snapshot)


Factory that builds containers
Dockerfile (build instructions)


Warehouse port
Registry (e.g. Docker Hub — where images are stored)



You do not need to know Linux, cloud computing, or programming to understand the idea of Docker. You only need to understand: same box → same result, anywhere.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
text:
Imagine you bake a cake using a recipe. You write down every ingredient, every step, and every oven temperature. If someone else follows the exact same recipe, they should get the exact same cake.
In science and medicine, we face a similar problem with software:

One person runs an analysis on a laptop and gets one result.
Another person runs the “same” analysis on a different computer and gets a different result — because their software versions, settings, or operating system are not identical.

Docker solves this by packaging software — and everything it needs to run — into a standard box that works the same way on almost any computer.






Tip



Simple definition: Docker lets you put an application and all its dependencies into a portable box called a container, so it runs the same way everywhere.




Before modern shipping, loading a ship was messy — every crate was a different size and shape.
Then standard shipping containers arrived. Any crane, truck, or ship built for containers could move any container, anywhere in the world.
Docker does the same for software:







Real world
Docker world




Shipping container
Container (running app)


Blueprint for the container
Image (frozen snapshot)


Factory that builds containers
Dockerfile (build instructions)


Warehouse port
Registry (e.g. Docker Hub — where images are stored)



You do not need to know Linux, cloud computing, or programming to understand the idea of Docker. You only need to understand: same box → same result, anywhere.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
section: 2. Why Docker matters in clinical, pharma & bio
text:
2. Why Docker matters in clinical, pharma & bio
If you work in clinical research, pharmaceutical companies, or biotechnology / bioinformatics, reproducibility and traceability are not optional — they are often required.

Clinical research & biostatistics
Examples where Docker helps:

Clinical trial analysis — TLFs (Tables, Listings, Figures) for a study should be produced with a fixed, documented R or SAS environment.
CDISC workflows — SDTM/ADaM conversion pipelines that must run identically for every database lock.
Independent verification — a statistician or QC reviewer can re-run analysis inside the same container and confirm results match.

Why it matters: Regulators and sponsors ask: “Can you prove this result was produced with a controlled, repeatable setup?” Docker is one practical way to answer yes.


Pharmaceutical industry
Examples:

Drug discovery pipelines — chemistry informatics, molecular docking, ML models for target identification.
Manufacturing & QC — image analysis, batch release calculations, stability study modeling.
Validated systems (GxP) — in regulated areas, teams document which exact software version was used. Container images can be versioned, tagged, and archived like a batch record.

Why it matters: In pharma, “it worked on my machine” is not acceptable for submissions, audits, or quality systems.


Biotechnology & bioinformatics
Examples:

Genomics pipelines — FASTQ → alignment → variant calling (same BWA, GATK, or bcftools versions every time).
Single-cell RNA-seq, proteomics, metabolomics — complex tool chains that are painful to install manually.
Cloud & HPC — run the same pipeline on a laptop for testing, then on AWS/GCP for production (see the Nextflow guide).

Why it matters: Bioinformatics tools change quickly. Docker pins the versions so a paper, pipeline, or clinical genomics report can be reproduced months or years later.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
section: 3. Key ideas — explained in plain language
text:
3. Key ideas — explained in plain language
You only need four words to start:

Image
An image is a frozen snapshot — like a saved recipe + all ingredients pre-measured and sealed.

It is read-only (you don’t edit it while it’s stored).
It has a name and tag, e.g. python:3.11 or rocker/r-ver:4.3.



Container
A container is a running instance of an image — like actually baking the cake.

You can start many containers from one image.
When you stop a container, its temporary changes are usually discarded (unless you save them).



Dockerfile
A Dockerfile is a text file with build instructions — step-by-step directions for creating an image.
Example (don’t worry if this looks technical — we explain each line below):
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY analysis.py .
CMD ["python", "analysis.py"]



Line
Plain English




FROM python:3.11-slim
Start from a pre-built Python 3.11 image


WORKDIR /app
Use /app as the working folder inside the box


COPY analysis.py .
Copy your script into the box


CMD [...]
When the container starts, run this command





Registry
A registry is an online library of images. Docker Hub is the most common public registry. Companies often use private registries for internal or validated images.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
section: 4. What you need before you start
text:
4. What you need before you start

On your computer

A laptop or desktop (Windows, Mac, or Linux)
Docker Desktop — free for personal/educational use







Important



You do not need to know Python, R, Linux, or cloud platforms to learn Docker basics. You only need to copy and run a few commands.




Install Docker Desktop

Go to https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop/
Download Docker Desktop for your operating system
Install it and open the Docker Desktop app
Wait until it says Docker is running (whale icon in the menu bar / system tray)



Check that Docker works
Open a terminal (Terminal on Mac, PowerShell or Command Prompt on Windows) and run:
docker --version
You should see something like Docker version 24.x.x.
Then run your first container:
docker run hello-world
If you see a friendly “Hello from Docker!” message — you succeeded.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
text:
5. Your first useful commands
Think of these as remote control buttons for containers.

See what is running
docker ps
Lists running containers. (Empty list is normal if nothing is running.)


See all containers (including stopped)
docker ps -a


Download and run a pre-built image
docker run -it rocker/r-ver:4.3 R --version
This downloads an R environment and prints the R version — without installing R on your laptop.
Why this matters in clinical/bio work: You can test an analysis in R 4.3 today, and a colleague in another country can run the identical R 4.3 environment tomorrow.


Run a command and exit
docker run --rm python:3.11-slim python -c "print('Analysis environment ready')"

--rm = delete the container when it finishes (keeps your machine tidy)



Stop a running container
docker stop CONTAINER_ID
(CONTAINER_ID comes from docker ps.)

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
section: 6. Hands-on example — a mini clinical-style analysis box
text:
6. Hands-on example — a mini clinical-style analysis box
This example is educational, not submission-ready — but it shows the pattern used in real projects.

Step 1: Create a folder
mkdir docker-clinical-demo
cd docker-clinical-demo


Step 2: Create a simple R script
Save this as summarize.R:
# Demo: summarize patient ages from a CSV
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)
input_file <- args[1]
output_file <- args[2]

dat <- read.csv(input_file)
result <- data.frame(
  n = nrow(dat),
  mean_age = mean(dat$age, na.rm = TRUE),
  min_age = min(dat$age, na.rm = TRUE),
  max_age = max(dat$age, na.rm = TRUE)
)
write.csv(result, output_file, row.names = FALSE)
cat("Summary written to", output_file, "\n")


Step 3: Create sample data
Save as patients.csv:
patient_id,age
001,54
002,61
003,47
004,58
005,52


Step 4: Create a Dockerfile
Save as Dockerfile:
FROM rocker/r-ver:4.3
WORKDIR /analysis
COPY summarize.R .
COPY patients.csv .
CMD ["Rscript", "summarize.R", "patients.csv", "summary.csv"]


Step 5: Build the image
docker build -t clinical-demo:1.0 .

-t clinical-demo:1.0 = name and version tag (like a batch number)
. = use the current folder



Step 6: Run the container
docker run --rm clinical-demo:1.0


Step 7: Copy the output file out (optional)
docker run --rm -v "$(pwd):/output" clinical-demo:1.0 \
  sh -c "Rscript summarize.R patients.csv /output/summary.csv"
On Windows PowerShell, use:
docker run --rm -v "${PWD}:/output" clinical-demo:1.0 sh -c "Rscript summarize.R patients.csv /output/summary.csv"
Open summary.csv — you should see mean, min, and max age.






Note



Industry pattern: The image tag (clinical-demo:1.0) documents which environment produced the output. In regulated settings, teams keep that tag in the analysis record.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
section: 7. Docker in pharma & clinical — real scenarios
text:
7. Docker in pharma & clinical — real scenarios

Scenario A: Reproducible TLF generation (clinical)
Problem: Two statisticians get slightly different p-values because one has an older R package.
Docker approach:

Build an image with fixed R + package versions.
Tag it study-abc-tlf-env:2024-03-15.
Every TLF run uses that tag.
QC re-runs the same tag to verify.



Scenario B: Bioinformatics pipeline (biotech)
Problem: Installing BWA, samtools, and GATK manually takes days and breaks on updates.
Docker approach:

Use community images (e.g. from Biocontainers).
Or build one image with all tools pinned.
Run the same image locally, on a server, or in the cloud.

docker run --rm biocontainers/fastqc:v0.11.9_cv8 fastqc --help


Scenario C: ML model for drug discovery (pharma)
Problem: A data scientist trains a model in Python; manufacturing wants to run it in a controlled environment.
Docker approach:

Package model + Python + libraries in an image.
Validate the image through your internal QA process.
Deploy the same image to staging and production.



Scenario D: Keeping sensitive data separate
Problem: Patient-level data must not leak between studies.
Docker approach:

Run each study in a separate container with separate mounted folders.
Never bake patient data into the image — mount it at run time only.
Combine with encryption, access controls, and audit logs (Docker alone is not enough for HIPAA/GDPR — it is one layer).

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
section: 8. GxP, validation & audit — what beginners should know
text:
8. GxP, validation & audit — what beginners should know
If you work in GxP (Good Practice guidelines — GCP, GLP, GMP), Docker is a tool, not a shortcut around validation.
Good practices:







Practice
Why




Version every image (:1.0, :2024-06-28)
Traceability — like a batch record


Document the Dockerfile
Shows how the environment was built


Store images in a controlled registry
Prevents unapproved changes


Test before production use
IQ/OQ-style checks on critical containers


Keep data out of images
Patient data belongs in secure storage, not baked into layers


Log who ran what
Combine Docker with workflow tools (Nextflow, Airflow, CI systems)









Warning



Docker helps with reproducibility and isolation. It does not replace SOPs, data privacy rules, 21 CFR Part 11 controls, or formal computer system validation on its own.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
section: 9. How Docker connects to other tools on this site
text:
9. How Docker connects to other tools on this site



Tool
How Docker fits




Nextflow
Runs each pipeline step inside a container — same tools everywhere


R / Clinical R
Fixed R + package versions for TLFs and CDISC work


Python
Data science and ML environments that match production


Cloud (AWS, GCP)
Same image on your laptop and in the cloud



One sentence: Docker is often the “standard box” that other tools carry your analysis in.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
text:
10. Common beginner mistakes (and fixes)







Mistake
Fix




“Docker is installed but commands fail”
Open Docker Desktop and wait until it is running


Putting secrets or patient data in a Dockerfile
Use mounted volumes and secret managers — never COPY PHI into an image


Using :latest tag in production
Always use specific version tags


Huge images (slow downloads)
Start from -slim base images; only install what you need


Expecting Docker to validate itself
Follow your organization’s QA / CSV process


Confusing image and container
Image = blueprint; container = running copy

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
text:
11. Quick reference — commands to remember
# Check Docker
docker --version

# First test
docker run hello-world

# List running containers
docker ps

# Build an image from a Dockerfile
docker build -t my-study-env:1.0 .

# Run an image
docker run --rm my-study-env:1.0

# Run with a folder mounted (data in/out)
docker run --rm -v "$(pwd):/data" my-study-env:1.0

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
text:
12. Next steps

Install Docker Desktop and run docker run hello-world.
Try the clinical demo above.
Explore Nextflow to see containers in multi-step pipelines.
Ask your IT team about private registries if you work in a regulated company.

title: Docker for Beginners — Clinical, Pharma & Life Sciences
text:
13. Further reading

Biocontainers — life-sciences tool images
